title: Statement by the Monetary Policy Board: Monetary Policy Decision
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia raised its cash rate target by 25 basis points to 4.10 percent, citing a material pickup in inflation during the second half of 2025 and upside risks to the inflation outlook. The Board noted that capacity pressures have increased, short-term inflation expectations have risen, and the Middle East conflict has led to sharply higher fuel prices. The decision was made by a majority vote of five members, with four members preferring to leave rates unchanged at 3.85 percent.
key_points: RBA increased cash rate target by 25 basis points to 4.10 percent from 3.85 percent
Inflation picked up materially in H2 2025 after falling from its 2022 peak
Middle East conflict has resulted in sharply higher fuel prices, adding to inflation pressure
Short-term measures of inflation expectations have risen, with Board judging material risk that inflation stays above target longer than anticipated
Labour market has tightened with unemployment slightly lower than expected and labour underutilisation at low rates
Vote split was 5-4 in favour of the 25bp increase
why_it_matters: This RBA rate hike signals that the central bank is actively responding to renewed inflationary pressures, including energy price shocks from the Middle East conflict and persistent domestic capacity pressures. The majority decision to tighten despite some dissenting members indicates the Board views upside inflation risks as significant. The policy direction contrasts with the rate reductions that occurred in 2025, representing a potential pivot in the monetary policy stance.
macro_context: The RBA is tightening policy in response to: (1) a material inflation pickup in H2 2025 driven by stronger-than-expected private demand, (2) energy price shocks from the Middle East conflict, and (3) a tightening labour market. This occurs as interest rate reductions in 2025 are still flowing through to the economy. Global monetary policy expectations have risen in Australia and most other advanced economies in response to Middle East conflict implications.
notes: Source is a primary RBA monetary policy statement, representing authoritative direct communication from the central bank. Content is directly relevant to macro catalysts through the rate decision and explicit inflation and growth analysis.
